note.soap:
S:
Patient reports tick bite. Reports a tick bite with a bullseye rash present. No systemic symptoms reported. Exposure occurred in Georgia.
O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. Patient describes characteristic erythema migrans rash consistent with bullseye appearance.

A:
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early localized Lyme disease (erythema migrans) given tick exposure in an endemic area, tick attachment >36 hours, and presence of bullseye rash.
  - Differential:
    1. Early Lyme disease
    2. Tick-bite hypersensitivity reaction
    3. Cellulitis or local skin infection
    4. Other tick-borne illnesses (less likely without systemic symptoms)
  - Red flags considered: No systemic symptoms (fever, malaise), no neurological or cardiac symptoms reported.

P:
  - Disposition: Urgent in-person evaluation recommended due to presence of erythema migrans rash and tick attachment >36 hours.
  - Plan: Patient should be evaluated promptly for clinical diagnosis confirmation and initiation of antibiotic therapy for Lyme disease per CDC guidelines. Consider doxycycline unless contraindicated. Educate patient on signs of progression or systemic symptoms.
  - Safety net: Advise patient to seek emergency care if fever, worsening rash, joint pain, neurological symptoms, or cardiac symptoms develop. Return for follow-up if symptoms persist or worsen.

Evidence (snippets used): CDC guidance recommends antibiotic treatment for Lyme disease when erythema migrans rash is present and tick attachment exceeds 36 hours in endemic areas.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
